edge: Visual reprogramming leverages the intrinsic capabilities of pretrained vision models by adapting their input or output interfaces to solve downstream tasks.
Visual reprogramming leverages pretrained vision models to solve downstream tasks with different labels.
When adapting the output interface, label mapping methods transform pretrained labels to downstream labels by establishing a gradient-free one-to-one correspondence between the two sets of labels.
However, one-to-one mappings may overlook the complex relationship between pretrained and downstream labels.
Motivated by the observation that one-to-one mappings overlook the complex relationship between labels, a Bayesian-guided Label Mapping method is proposed.
BLM constructs a probabilistic label mapping matrix to quantifying pairwise relationships between pretrained and downstream labels.

The assignment of values to the constructed matrix is guided by Bayesian conditional probability, considering the joint distribution of downstream labels and the labels predicted by the pretrained model.
Experiments demonstrate the superior performance of BLM over existing methods for pretrained vision models.
Experiments demonstrate the superior performance of BLM for vision-language models.
The success of BLM offers a probabilistic lens to understand and analyze the effectiveness of Visual reprogramming.
The probabilistic lens is used to understand and analyze the effectiveness of VR.

Pretrained models are repurposed for downstream tasks.
A label mapping function is needed to map outputs of pretrained models to downstream labels.
The label mapping function avoids the computational cost of training fully-connected output layers through backpropagation.
Existing VR methods adopt a gradient-free one-to-one label mapping function.
One-to-one label mapping overlooks the many-to-many relationship between pretrained and downstream labels.
The complex relationship between pretrained and downstream labels may limit the performance of VR.

The one-to-one LM function retains only the label with the highest logit, ignoring the probabilities of other related labels.
The probabilistic LM matrix replaces the one-to-one LM function, allowing predictions to consider diverse contributions from all pretrained labels.
The probabilistic LM matrix quantifies the relationship between a pretrained label and a downstream label, enabling a many-to-many mapping strategy.
The Bayesian-guided label mapping assigns values to elements in the probabilistic LM matrix based on Bayesian conditional probabilities.
The joint distribution is calculated from the predicted pretrained labels on downstream tasks.
The joint distribution is calculated from the ground-truth downstream labels.
BLM is further extended to BLM+, which aggregates top-K predicted probabilities instead of using a single predicted label.
BLM+ accounts for uncertainty in the predictions when estimating the joint distribution.
The theoretical analysis justifies that probabilistic many-to-many LM has the potential to outperform deterministic one-to-one LM.

Both BLM and BLM+ are applied to different input VR methods (padding and watermarking) on pretrained ResNet and ResNeXt.

Compared to current gradient-free LM methods, the deep learning-based LM may still have an advantage in the performance of downstream tasks due to the learning capacity of the linear layer neural network with respect to BLM.
Our proposed BLM and BLM+ aim to bridge the gap between gradient-free LM and deep learning-based LM.
BLM and BLM+ have been observed to possess greater interpretability and fewer parameters compared to deep learning-based LM.

BLM can be applied to different input VR methods, overlaying trainable noise patterns on resized images, and outperforms ILM in accuracy by 6.1% and 7.5%, respectively. BLM and BLM+ close the gap between current gradient-free and deep learning-based LM in the case of watermarking-based VR.

BLM+ can be applied to different input VR methods, overlaying trainable noise patterns on resized images, and outperforms ILM in accuracy by 6.1% and 7.5%, respectively. BLM and BLM+ close the gap between current gradient-free and deep learning-based LM in the case of watermarking-based VR.
ResNet-18 is used as the pretrained model for BLM in watermarking-based VR, where BLM outperforms the best-performing baseline ILM in accuracy.
ResNet-18 is used as the pretrained model for BLM+ in watermarking-based VR, where BLM+ outperforms the best-performing baseline ILM in accuracy.
BLM is applied on vision-language models like CLIP, achieving an average accuracy of 79.1% on 12 datasets and outperforming the baseline methods on 11 datasets.
